results.possibleIdentity: David Glasser is an architect and artist known for his significant contributions to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) collective during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was a key member of the core group of artists and engineers who designed and realized the Pepsi Pavilion for the 1970 World Exposition (Expo '70) in Osaka, Japan. This project was a landmark collaboration that integrated art, technology, and environmental design, featuring a giant spherical mirror and atmospheric fog. Glasser's work within E.A.T. often bridged the gap between structural architecture and experimental art. He is also recognized as a contributor to the 1972 book 'Pavilion,' which documents the E.A.T. Osaka project. Professionally, he served as a professor of architecture and was the head of the Community Development Center at the University of Arkansas, where he led urban renewal and reconstruction efforts, such as the rebuilding of Arkadelphia after a tornado in 1997. He eventually retired from the University of Arkansas in 2003 and relocated to Turkey.

David Glasser is a prominent figure in the British art world, currently serving as the Executive Chair and Chief Executive of the Ben Uri Gallery and Museum in London. Although he identifies as an artist and curator, his primary career was in the corporate sector at Marks & Spencer and Ward White Group, where he was responsible for pioneering retail concepts. He transitioned from his business career in 2001 to lead the re-invigoration of Ben Uri, transforming it into a digital-led museum focused on the immigrant and refugee contribution to British art. While he is active in the art world and has curated numerous exhibitions, his tenure in a leadership role within the arts post-dates the 1960s and 70s timeframe, as he was building his corporate career during that period.

David Glasser is an award-winning audio mastering engineer and the founder of Airshow Mastering, based in Boulder, Colorado. Active since the mid-1970s, he began his career in college radio at Brandeis University (WBRS) and later worked for the Boston Symphony and National Public Radio (NPR). He is widely known for his high-fidelity restoration and mastering work on archival recordings from the 1960s and 1970s, most notably for the Grateful Dead. While he is a technical artist in the field of audio engineering and works extensively with recordings from the era mentioned, he is generally categorized as a mastering engineer rather than a visual or conceptual artist associated with the E.A.T. organization.

results.possibleIdentity: Denys George Irving (1944–1976), also known as Dennis Irving, was a Welsh-born filmmaker, computer animation pioneer, and experimental musician. He was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, moving between New York and London. While studying at Columbia University in the late 1960s, he used the university's mainframe computer to develop early computer-generated silent films. He also pioneered psychedelic projection systems using liquid inks on glass slides. In 1968, under the name Dennis Irving, he recorded a notable interview with the experimental jazz composer Sun Ra for Pacifica Radio (WBAI). Upon returning to London in 1969, he founded Lucifer Films Ltd and produced solo rock music under the project name 'Lucifer,' releasing LPs such as 'Big Gun' and 'Exit' in the early 1970s. His work has been recognized in the history of British artists' film and video and has been exhibited at the Tate Gallery.

Denis Irving (active 1950s–2000s) was a prominent Australian theatre technical consultant, lighting designer, and acoustic specialist based in Melbourne. He was a central figure in the technical production era of the 1960s and 1970s, serving as a technical director and consultant for the Melbourne Theatre Company (MTC). His work often involved complex collaborations between artistic vision and engineering, such as redesigning theatre technical specifications and proposing innovative fire safety systems that replaced traditional fire curtains with stage ventilation. He worked as a lighting designer for major productions, including early shows for Barry Humphries (Dame Edna Everage) at the University of Melbourne's Union Theatre in the mid-1950s. His legacy in the performing arts is honored through the Dennis Irving Scholarship, which supports sound designers at the Melbourne Theatre Company.
